1: The regnal formula for Zedekiah: twenty-one years old when he became king, eleven-year reign, mother Hamutal daughter of Jeremiah of Libnah. The chapter is a near-verbatim parallel of 2 Kings 24:18-25:30, serving as the historical confirmation that all of Jeremiah's oracles came to pass. Its presence here makes Jeremiah 52 both an appendix and a verification.
2: wayyaʿăśeh hārāʿ bĕʿênê YHWH kĕkōl ʾăšer-ʿāśâ Yĕhôyāqîm — "He did what was evil in the sight of the LORD, just as Jehoiakim had done." The evaluative formula aligns Zedekiah with the worst of Judah's kings. The comparison to Jehoiakim (who cut and burned Baruch's scroll, Jeremiah 36) is pointed: Zedekiah, despite consulting Jeremiah repeatedly, ultimately followed Jehoiakim's path of covenantal disobedience.
3: kî ʿal-ʾap-YHWH hāyĕtâ bîrûšālaim ûbîhûdâ — "For it was because of the LORD's anger that all this happened to Jerusalem and Judah." The causal statement of the fall: not Babylonian military superiority but YHWH's covenantal anger. ʿAd-hišlîkô ʾōtām mēʿal pānāyw — "until he finally expelled them from his presence." The expulsion-from-presence language echoes Eden — the fall of Jerusalem as a covenant expulsion parallel to the primal expulsion.
4: The siege begins: ninth year of Zedekiah, tenth month, tenth day — the precise dating matches Ezekiel 24:1-2 where YHWH tells Ezekiel the date of the siege's start at the exact moment it begins. The precision is both historical record and theological witness to prophetic foreknowledge.
5: The siege lasted until the eleventh year of Zedekiah — a two-year siege. The duration is noted without elaboration; the details of the famine (Jeremiah 52:6) and the wall-breach (Jeremiah 52:7) fill in the human suffering. The brevity of "the city remained under siege" (wattābōʾ hāʿîr bammātsôr) contrasts with the catastrophic consequences that follow.
6: wayĕhî baḥōdeš hārĕbîʿî bĕtiśʿâ laḥōdeš wayĕḥězaq hārāʿāb bāʿîr — "By the ninth day of the fourth month the famine in the city had become so severe that there was no food left for the people of the land." The famine — the covenantal curse of Deuteronomy 28:52-57 — reaches its extremity. Lĕḥem lāʿam hāʾārets (bread for the common people) — the hierarchy of suffering is inverted: even the basic food of ordinary people is exhausted.
7: The breach in the city wall (nĕbqĕʿâ hāʿîr) and the flight of the soldiers at night. The gate between the two walls (baššaʿar bên haḥōmōtayim) — the soldiers flee through the narrow passage between Zion's double walls. The Chaldeans surrounded the city. The MKT renders "the gate between the two walls" to preserve the archaeological detail of Jerusalem's fortification system.
8: wayyirdĕpû ḥêl-Kaśdîm ʾaḥărê hammelek wayyaśśîgû ʾet-Ṣidqiyyāhû — "But the Chaldean army pursued the king and caught up with Zedekiah in the plains of Jericho." The plains of Jericho — the route toward the Jordan and potential escape east — was the flight path. All his troops scattered (wayyāpĕṣû mēʾittô kol-ḥêlô). The king who failed to defend Jerusalem is captured fleeing alone.
9: Zedekiah captured, brought to Nebuchadnezzar at Riblah (Riblāh) in Hamath — the Syrian headquarters of Babylonian military command. Sentence passed on him (waydabbēr ʾittô mišpāṭîm). The legal vocabulary: mišpāṭ (judgment, sentence) — the covenant judge judges through the pagan king. Jeremiah had warned Zedekiah explicitly that this was coming if he did not surrender (Jeremiah 38:17-18).
10: wayyišḥaṭ melek-Bābel ʾet-bĕnê Ṣidqiyyāhû lĕʿênāyw — "The king of Babylon killed Zedekiah's sons before his eyes." The fulfillment of Jeremiah 34:3-4's oracle: Jeremiah had said Zedekiah would see the king of Babylon eye to eye. The last sight Zedekiah has before his blinding is the murder of his sons. The horror is the oracle's precise fulfillment. "Had all the officials of Judah executed at Riblah" — the institutional leadership eliminated.
11: wĕʾet-ʿênê Ṣidqiyyāhû ʿiwwēr wayyaʾassrēhû bĕnaḥuštayim — "Then he blinded Zedekiah, bound him in bronze shackles." The blinding and binding fulfills the specific oracle of Jeremiah 32:5: "he will lead Zedekiah to Babylon." Zedekiah dies in prison in Babylon (bêt-happĕquddōt ʿad-yôm mōtô) — the precise fate Jeremiah announced. The MKT renders "bronze shackles" for naḥuštayim — the material (bronze) is specified.
12: The fifth month, tenth day, nineteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar — the precise date of the temple's burning. Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard (rab-ṭabbāḥîm, chief of the executioners/bodyguard) as the Babylonian officer carrying out the final destruction. The MKT renders rab-ṭabbāḥîm as "captain of the guard" following standard convention while noting the literal meaning involves slaughtering.
13: wayyiśrōp ʾet-bêt-YHWH wĕʾet-bêt hammelek — "He set fire to the house of the LORD, the royal palace, and all the houses of Jerusalem — every important building he burned down." The sentence is stark and comprehensive: temple, palace, all important buildings — the complete annihilation of the Solomonic city. The burning of the temple is the pivotal trauma of the OT — the loss that reshapes all subsequent Judaism and gives Lamentations its occasion.
14: The entire Chaldean army tears down all the walls surrounding Jerusalem (kol-ḥômôt Yĕrûšālaim sābîb nātĕṣû). The wall-demolition confirms the siege's completion: no defensive perimeter remains. The MKT renders sābîb (round about, on all sides) to emphasize the comprehensive demolition of Jerusalem's fortifications.
15: The deportees: some of the poorest of the people (miḏallôt hāʿām), survivors remaining in the city, those who had gone over to the king of Babylon, and the rest of the artisans (yeter hāʾāmôn). The social mix of the deportees — poor, survivors, defectors, craftsmen — reflects the indiscriminate sweep of the deportation. The MKT follows the list as given.
16: Contrast with Jeremiah 52:15: Nebuzaradan left behind the very poorest of the land (miḏallôt hāʾārets) to work the vineyards and fields. The agricultural remnant — those too economically marginal to be useful as skilled labor in Babylon — are left on the land. Their agricultural function preserved the land's productivity for Babylonian benefit.
17: wĕʾēt ʿammûdê hanneḥōšet ʾăšer bêt-YHWH — the bronze inventory begins. The pillars (Jachin and Boaz of 1 Kings 7:15-22), the movable stands (mĕkônôt), and the bronze sea (hayyām hanneḥōšet) — the major bronze installations of Solomon's temple — are dismantled and taken. The deliberate cataloguing of the lost objects parallels the cataloguing of the tabernacle components in Exodus: what was carefully listed in construction is now carefully listed in destruction.
18: The secondary bronze implements: pots (sîrôt), shovels (yaʿîm), snuffers (mĕzammĕrôt), sprinkling bowls (mizrāqôt), incense dishes (kappôt). All the bronze utensils used in the temple service are taken. The specificity of the list serves theological witness: these are not generic valuables but the instruments of covenant worship, now in Babylonian hands.
19: Gold and silver items: small bowls (hassippîm), fire pans (hamaghtôt), basins (mizrāqôt), pots (sîrôt), lampstands (mĕnōrôt), incense dishes (kappôt), and drink offering bowls (qĕśāwôt). The captain of the guard carried away all items — gold ones (gold) and silver ones (silver). The tautology "those made of gold, in gold; those made of silver, in silver" emphasizes the comprehensive precious-metal appropriation.
20: The summary of the major bronze pieces: the two pillars, the single sea with its twelve bronze bulls underneath, and the movable stands. "Which King Solomon had made for the LORD's temple — the bronze of all these vessels was beyond weighing." The immeasurability of the bronze underscores Solomon's achievement and the magnitude of the loss. The MKT preserves "beyond weighing" for ʾên mišqāl (no weight/measuring).
21: Dimensions of the pillars: eighteen cubits tall (c. 27 feet), twelve cubits in circumference (c. 18 feet), four fingers thick and hollow. The precision of the measurements serves as witness — this is documented loss, not myth. The MKT follows the MT measurements, converting to an approximation: the pillars were substantial structures whose dimensions were worth recording.
22: The bronze capital (wĕkōteret) on top of each pillar: five cubits high, decorated with latticework (śĕbākâ) and pomegranates (rimmônîm) all around. The decorative elements — pomegranates and network — were the specific adornment described in 1 Kings 7:17-20. The MKT follows the detailed description as inventory record.
23: The pomegranate count: ninety-six visible on the sides, one hundred total on the surrounding latticework of each pillar. The numerical precision (96 on sides, 100 total) suggests an actual count at removal. This level of exactness — counting pomegranates — is the narrator's way of insisting on the reality of the loss: these are real objects, not symbols, and they were really taken.
24: wayyiqqaḥ rab-ṭabbāḥîm ʾet-Śĕrāyāhû kōhēn hārōʾš — "The captain of the guard arrested Seraiah the chief priest, Zephaniah the deputy priest, and the three gatekeepers." The same Seraiah who received Jeremiah's scroll oracle (Jeremiah 51:59) is now arrested. The three gatekeepers (šōmĕrê hassap) — the temple's security personnel. The MKT renders kōhēn mišneh as "deputy priest" — the second priest in the temple hierarchy.
25: The city officials taken: one military officer, seven royal advisers (šibʿâ ʾănāšîm of the king's council), the secretary who mustered troops (hassōpēr śar haṣṣābāʾ), and sixty citizens of the people (šišším ʾîš). The administrative infrastructure of the fallen city is systematically arrested. The MKT renders the titles distinctly: officer, advisers, secretary, citizens.
26: Nebuzaradan takes all of them to Nebuchadnezzar at Riblah. The Babylonian chain of command is preserved: the officer Nebuzaradan executes the arrests; the king Nebuchadnezzar at his headquarters in Hamath pronounces the sentences. Power is concentrated; judgment is formal. The MKT preserves both geographic names (Riblah, Hamath) as markers of Babylonian imperial geography.
27: wayyakkēm melek-Bābel waymîtēm bĕRiblâ bĕʾerets Ḥāmāt — "There the king of Babylon had them all struck down and executed at Riblah in the land of Hamath." The execution of the leadership closes the narrative of the fall. Wayyigel Yĕhûdâ mēʿal ʾadmātô — "So Judah was carried away from its land." The concluding statement of exile — the covenantal expulsion formula. The MKT renders gālâ (exile/carry into captivity) as "carried away."
28: zeh hāʿām ʾăšer heglâ Nĕbûkadreʾṣar — the deportation census begins. First deportation: seventh year of Nebuchadnezzar, 3,023 Judeans. The number 3,023 is specific enough to suggest official records. This is the deportation after Jehoiachin's surrender (2 Kings 24:10-16), which the kings account numbers as 10,000 (counting all social classes). The 3,023 may be the number of specifically Judean adult males. The MKT renders the numbers as given in the MT.
29: Second deportation: eighteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar, 832 persons. The smaller number (832 vs 3,023) for the main deportation after the fall of Jerusalem is striking — it may count only certain categories of deportees (craftsmen, officials) rather than the total. The discrepancy between the census here and the accounts in 2 Kings has generated extensive discussion; the MKT follows the Jeremiah figure without harmonizing.
30: Third deportation: twenty-third year of Nebuchadnezzar, Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard deported 745 Judeans. This deportation is not mentioned in Kings — it may be connected to the events following the assassination of Gedaliah (Jeremiah 40-41). Total: 4,600. The three-wave deportation census is unique to Jeremiah's appendix. The small total (4,600) compared to the larger estimates elsewhere may indicate that only certain categories of deportees are counted in each wave.
31: wayhî bišlōšîm wĕšebaʿ šānâ lĕgālût Yĕhôyākîn melek-Yĕhûdâ — "In the thirty-seventh year of Jehoiachin king of Judah's exile, on the twenty-fifth day of the twelfth month, Evil-merodach king of Babylon in the year he became king... lifted up the head of Jehoiachin." The book's final episode: Jehoiachin released and honored under Evil-merodach (Amel-Marduk, 562-560 BC). The phrase nāśāʾ ʾet-rōʾš Yĕhôyākîn (lifted up his head) — nāśāʾ rōʾš means both to lift the head (honor) and appears in Genesis 40:13 for Pharaoh's release of the cupbearer. The MKT renders "lifted up the head of Jehoiachin" preserving the idiom.
32: waydabbēr ʾittô ṭōbôt — "He treated him with respect (ṭōbôt, good things) and gave him a seat of honor above the other captive kings in Babylon." The elevation above other captive kings shows political rehabilitation — Jehoiachin is treated as a legitimate king in the Babylonian court, not merely a prisoner. This detail was confirmed by the Weidner Tablets (Babylonian ration tablets) discovered in Berlin which list oil rations for "Yaukin king of Judah" and his sons.
33: wĕhēlîp ʾēt bigdê kilʾô ûʾākal leḥem lĕpānāyw tāmîd — "So Jehoiachin changed out of his prison clothes and ate regularly at the king's table for the rest of his life." Prison clothes exchanged for palace clothes — the restoration of dignity through clothing. Leḥem... tāmîd (bread/food... regularly) — the covenant-provision formula: sustained at the king's table. The MKT renders "for the rest of his life" (kol-yĕmê ḥayyāyw) to mark the permanence of the rehabilitation.
34: wĕʾarḥātô ʾarḥat tāmîd nittĕnâ-lô mēʾēt melek-Bābel dĕbar-yôm bĕyômô ʿad-yôm mōtô — "As for his food allowance, a regular daily portion was granted him by the king of Babylon, every day until his death." The book ends with a daily food allowance for the Davidic heir — a small mercy extended by a pagan king. The ending is deliberately quiet: no restoration of the kingdom, no return to Jerusalem, just daily bread from a foreign king. Yet the Davidic line survives; the covenant is not extinguished. The MKT renders dĕbar-yôm bĕyômô (the matter of a day in its day) as "every day" — the idiom for daily provision. Jeremiah ends where it began: in the shadow of Babylon, with a remnant alive, and the covenant word still in force.
